data.title: What To Do After Another Bumble Stumble?
data.description:
I started adding shares of Bumble Inc. (NASDAQ:BMBL) to my portfolio in the low-mid teens in late 2023. I've long been doubtful about the durability of online dating platforms (what used to work in the dating world often doesn't work anymore), and I was specifically trigger-shy coming out of the pandemic. Just as people plotted "revenge travel" after being stuck in their own cities for an extended period, I felt that a period of "revenge dating" (not to mean an attempt to get revenge on your ex) was also likely to occur. I reasoned that the last thing people would want to do is sit online and swipe profiles when they had spent nearly 100% of their time online for a couple of years.

Nonetheless, Bumble appealed to me as an investor for a couple of reasons. First, it remained differentiated due to the platform's female-first contact structure. Second, I was weak at the knees for the company's $150m+ of annual free cash flow.

I remained a shareholder as the bottom dropped out from the stock in early-August 2024. Shares fell by as much as 40% on August 8, and I was likely one of the few investors who picked up more shares of BMBL below $5 that day (the stock closed at $5.71). I didn't see enough weakness in those Q2 results to invalidate the free cash flow thesis I had been clinging to. Ultimately, it paid off to average down on the panic.

I layered out of my trade in several chunks, the last of which was in the $9 range. I counted my lucky stars to have not realized a big net loss. Giving up on the cash flow story wasn't easy, especially as other analysts relished it, but I simply didn't have enough confidence in a business rebound. Ultimately, I reallocated my capital to better opportunities.

I was certainly tempted to layer back into a BMBL position as the stock dipped into the mid-$7 range in January, but never ended up pulling the trigger. Given the company's Q4 results and 2025 guidance released on February 18th, 2025, I'm pretty happy that I didn't.

Bumble's Q4 Results and Guidance Deliver Another Disappointment

On Tuesday, Bumble reported their Q4 results (or most of them). Revenue fell more than -4% for the quarter as compared to Q4 2023, and that's the worst comparable top-line result the company has seen. Expectations were pretty low, though, and Seeking Alpha reports that revenue slightly beat consensus.

Interestingly, Bumble was not able to provide a net earnings result for Q4 and FY 2024 due to what it described as accounting for foreign currency impacts had not yet been finalized. Whatever the ultimate result, it's an incremental concern that the company could not file a complete set of numbers.

What we do know is that profitability weakened, as average users increased about +6% against a ~9% decrease in average revenue per user.

As Bumble likes to present Adjusted EBITDA numbers, I'm always keen to have a look at the reconciliation tape, but the details aren't fully available for Q4 2024. Thankfully, Bumble did provide an operating income number of $36.97 million and an adjusted EBITDA of $72.51 million for the quarter. However, I fully consider Stock-Based Compensation [SBC] a real cost, so I'm not going to buy this adjusted EBITDA number. Instead, I come to a result of $58.53 million for the quarter, and $277.86 million for the year. On that basis, the deterioration in Q4 becomes more clear.

The guidance provided by the company in their conference call is also very disappointing. For FY 2025, management expects a revenue decline of 7%-10%. Margins are also expected to suffer. On the basis of the company's adjusted EBITDA calculation (which I don't agree with), their EBITDA margin for Q4 was about 27.7%. Management is expecting the apples-to-apples EBITDA margin for Q1 2025 to come in around 25%, which would be a decline of some 270 basis points. CFO Anu Subramanian attributed the expect decline to both revenue headwinds as well as investment costs. Earlier in the call, outgoing CEO Lidiane Jones indicated the company had new plans to innovate, and obviously some increased costs will be attributed there.

Cash Flow Statement Guesswork

I'm disappointed (and surprised) that Bumble did not provide cash flow information for the recent quarter. In the SEC filing that was available, the company stated that the delayed finalization of foreign currency impacts would have no impact on cash flows, yet the company failed to provide the cash flow details for Q4.

The company did report that its cash balance as of December 31, 2024, was $204.3 million and that total debt was $617.1 million, for a net debt balance of $412.8 million. However, these numbers seem awfully surprising.

As of September 30, 2024, I see Bumble had net debt of around $365 million. This would suggest that net debt increased by $48 million over Q4! The company did spend $40.3 million on share repurchases over the quarter, but that still doesn't get us back to even.

Based on these totals, it looks like Bumble either significantly ramped up its capex in Q4 or may have experienced negative operating cash flow during Q4 2024 due potentially to working capital adjustments.

Whatever occurred at the bank in Q4 seems to raise significant doubt about the cash flow story that had driven my previous investment interest.

Valuation

You'll forgive me for using back-of-the-napkin estimates as I try to figure out BMBL's valuation on a forward basis.

Using my own adjusted EBITDA number for Q4 2024 (which removed SBC add back) and then factoring in a top-line decline of 8% and a margin decline of 270 bps (as guided for Q1) brings me to an approximate EV/EBITDA of about 5.8x based on BMBL's February 19 closing price of $5.70.

This multiple remains a substantial discount to where Match Group (MTCH) is trading. However, I'm working with a number of unknown variables.

Concluding Remarks and Recommendation

Bumble's Q4 2024 release has seemingly left investors with a lot of disappointment and open questions, including:

  • What are the "innovations" that Bumble plans to introduce in an attempt to revitalize its business?
  • Will Bumble's adjusted EBITDA bottom in Q1 2025 at 25%, or will it continue to run at that level or lower through 2025?
  • How much did Bumble spend on capex in Q4 2024, and does this explain the surprising increase in net debt?
  • How should investors react to the company's failure to deliver full audited financials for Q4 2024?
  • What will the return of founder Whitney Wolfe Herd mean for Bumble's future, and has the company "kitchen-sinked" Q4 and sandbagged guidance to offer her an easy baseline?

Shares of BMBL fell nearly -20% in after-hours trading on February 18th but lost an additional $1 during the Feb 19th trading session, following the (partial) results and forward guidance. I'm not at all surprised by the very negative session after investors had the chance to unpack the information (and missing information) released by Bumble.

I wouldn't be surprised if some Wall Street analysts simply suspend their price targets for BMBL amidst the growing list of unknowns.

Using my back-of-the-napkin (my adjusted) EV/EBITDA multiple of ~5.8x based on BMBL's after-hours trading price, at face value BMBL may look reasonably priced for investors with a value/contrarian mindset.

With that said, the whole story looks substantially worse than it did back in August 2024. That's when the stock briefly slipped below $5. Therefore, I, personally, can't imagine taking a flyer on BMBL unless shares trade meaningfully below that level. The best hope for the bulls is that Bumble has given Ms. Wolfe Herd a story she can't make worse. I could see myself perhaps making a gamble on Bumble if shares drop below $4.

To therefore answer the "what to do?" question in the title, I think the answer is Sell, or Pray.

Manager Commentary

“Looking ahead to 2025, we believe market dynamics appear poised for rotation. While elevated valuations and narrow leadership raise caution, we think opportunities may emerge in value, cyclicals, and small caps as economic momentum broadens.” - Westfield Capital Management Company, L.P.

Market In Review

The S&P 500 reached 57 record highs in 2024, marking one of its best years since 1928, with over 20% annual returns for the second consecutive year. This remarkable performance was fueled by resilient U.S. economic growth, easing inflation, and enthusiasm for artificial intelligence (“AI”)-driven innovation. Large-cap growth stocks, led by the "Magnificent 7," continued to dominate, while small caps posted their worst relative performance since 1998. The U.S. Federal Reserve’s (“Fed”) pivot to rate cuts, along with optimism surrounding deregulation and fiscal reforms following the election, added momentum to a strong year, despite persistent concerns about market breadth and valuation extremes.

Portfolio Performance

During the fourth quarter of 2024, the Harbor Small Cap Growth Fund (Institutional Class, “Fund”) returned –2.87%, underperforming the Russell 2000® Growth Index, which returned 1.70%.

From a sector perspective, Consumer Discretionary, Information Technology, and Industrials were the primary detractors from relative performance. The underperformance was due to a combination of stock-specific weakness and common factor headwinds. From a factor perspective, the Fund’s underweight exposure to volatility and overweight exposure to size was a relative headwind to performance, which offset a relative tailwind from being underweight leverage.

Contributors & Detractors

Celestica (CLS), an electronics manufacturing services company, was the top contributor during the quarter after reporting a beat and raise in its third-quarter earnings report, as the company has benefited from its exposure to AI hyperscale customers. We sold our position during the quarter after the stock hit our internal price target.

Procore Technologies (PCOR), a cloud-based construction management software company, also contributed positively to relative returns after the company reset 2025 financial expectations and subsequently introduced new, long-term margin targets above expectations. We continue to favor Procore based on the potential for growth reacceleration and significant margin expansion.

Option Care Health (OPCH), a home health care and infusion provider, was the top detractor from relative returns during the quarter. The stock sold off following the company’s third-quarter earnings report in which management indicated a faster-than-anticipated decline in profit margin when a large portfolio drug goes generic in 2025. Despite the recent headline controversy, we maintain conviction in the company’s advantaged position as the only national home infusion provider that is taking market share in a growing industry, with the potential for additional upside from Alzheimer's drug-at-home infusions.

During the quarter, Vaxcyte (PCVX), a novel vaccine developer, also detracted from relative performance after the stock sold off over concerns about a new political administration and the potential impact on vaccine companies. In our view, the new administration will not have any impact on Vaxcyte’s franchise, and we believe the company could have a direct line of sight to a long-duration, total addressable market, which might represent a strategic asset for Big Pharma vaccine players.

Buys & Sells

During the quarter, we purchased Genius Sports (GENI), a leader in sports data analytics for global sports leagues and betting operators. Genius operates in a duopoly industry with secular tailwinds from the sports betting industry's 10% compound annual growth rate. We believe the company should benefit from long-term, exclusive contracts (including a multi-year NFL extension) and is approaching profitability, supported by its high-margin BetVision platform and growing advertising segment.

During the quarter, we sold Ciena (CIEN), a network and communication infrastructure company, after the stock hit our internal price target. In addition, the communication equipment space had a big move, and we were worried about fiscal year guidance in the new year.

Sector Overweights & Underweights

Financials represented the Fund’s largest overweight relative to the index at the end of 2023 and continues to be the largest overweight as of year-end 2024. Our exposure is heavily weighted toward growth-at-a-reasonable-price investment opportunities with idiosyncratic growth stories and limited interest rate risk. Across the sector, we are overweight property and casualty insurance with exposure to a few niche stories, modestly overweight banks, and underweight asset managers.

Consumer Staples represented the Fund’s largest underweight at the end of 2023 and continues to be the largest underweight as of year-end 2024. We have historically struggled to identify exciting growth opportunities in this group, and the resultant underweight has been characteristic of Fund positioning over time.

Outlook

For 2025, we believe market dynamics appear poised for rotation. While elevated valuations and narrow leadership raise caution, we think opportunities may emerge in value, cyclicals, and small caps as economic momentum broadens. The Fed’s accommodative stance, coupled with fiscal stimulus, support a constructive outlook in our view, but risks surrounding inflation and policy missteps remain key concerns. For these reasons, we believe a disciplined approach emphasizing high-quality investments and valuation sensitivity will be essential to navigating this evolving landscape.

Allocation Effect is the portion of portfolio excess return that is attributable to taking different group bets from the benchmark. (If either the portfolio or the benchmark has no position in a given group, allocation effect is the lone effect.) A group's allocation effect equals the average percent capitalization of the portfolio's group minus the average percent cap of the benchmark's group times the total return of the benchmark group minus the total return of the benchmark.

Average Weight is the dollar value (price times the shares held) of the security or group, divided by the total dollar value of the entire portfolio displayed as a percentage. It is calculated as the simple arithmetic average of daily values.

Contribution to Return is the contribution of a security or group to the overall portfolio return. It is calculated as the security weight multiplied by the daily security return linked daily across the reporting period. Currency Contribution is Total Return in USD subtracting out the Local Returns.

Local Returns are the Total Return of the portfolio or benchmark using the local currency.

Selection Effect is the portion of portfolio excess return attributable to choosing different securities within groups from the benchmark. A group's security selection effect equals the average weight of the benchmark's group times the total return of the portfolio's group minus the total return of the benchmark's group.

Total Effect is the sum of Allocation Effect and Selection Effect. The total effect represents the opportunity cost of what was done in a group relative to the overall portfolio. It is not just the difference between percent contribution in the portfolio and benchmark. At the overall portfolio level, the two numbers are equal. At the group level, they can be different.

Total Return is the price change of a security or group including dividends accrued over the report period (or the in-portfolio return) which includes only the time period that each security was in the portfolio.

Definitions

Beta is a measure of systematic risk, or the sensitivity of a fund to movements in the benchmark. A beta of 1 implies that the expected movement of a fund's return would match that of the benchmark used to measure beta.

Median Market Cap: The median size of the companies in a portfolio or index as measured by the market value of outstanding shares.

Weighted Average Market Capitalization: The average size of the companies in a portfolio or index as measured by the market value of outstanding shares. Earnings per share is the portion of a company's profit allocated to each outstanding share.

The Est 3-5 Yr EPS Growth (%) is the estimated growth of earnings per share over the next 3-5 years, using pre-calculated mean long-term EPS growth rate estimates, which are calculated using each individual broker’s methodology, from FactSet, First Call, I/B/E/S Consensus, and Reuters. Forward-looking estimates may not come to pass.

% EPS Growth - Past 3 Year: Earnings per share refers to the bottom-line measure of a company’s profitability defined as net income divided by the number of outstanding shares. The Adjusted Trailing P/E (price-to-earnings) Ratio is the closing stock price divided by the sum of the last 12 months actual EPS.

Forecasted P/E Ratio: a measure of the P/E (price-to-earnings) ratio using forecasted earnings for the P/E calculation. Return on Equity is a measure of financial performance calculated by dividing net income by shareholders' equity. The Price/Book (price-to-book) Ratio evaluates a firm's market value relative to its book value.

All P/E, ROE and P/B statistics are calculated as weighted medians.

Manager Commentary (As of 12/31/2024)

“As in the previous two years, we continue to see revenue and profits gains for Fund holdings growing at faster rates than the averages against which we compare, while the Fund enjoys a greater degree of diversification and less concentration.” - Jennison Associates, LLC

Market in Review

U.S. equities closed out a strong fourth quarter to end the year near record levels. The quarter’s gains came as voters went to the polls in early November and chose to return Donald Trump to the White House. Markets then moved higher, reflecting enthusiasm over campaign promises from the president-elect to relax regulatory oversight targeted initially at the Financials and Energy sectors. At the same time, investors faced the prospects of increasing tariffs and greater trade friction because of the election. Yields on longer-term U.S. government bonds moved higher in response to proposals about memorializing the original Trump administration tax cuts and increased deductions for state and local taxes, as well as potentially further miscellaneous tax reductions, but without concrete plans to raise offsetting revenue. The U.S. 10-year yield closed out 2024 at ≈4.5% versus ≈3.9% at the start of the year. For the period, the U.S. dollar gained against other currencies on the presumption that the new president’s “America First” agenda likely comes at the expense of growth in other parts of the globe.

Moreover, the pace of U.S. economic activity continued its moderating trend. The U.S. Federal Reserve (“Fed”), in response, trimmed an additional 0.25% from its target fed funds rate to 4.25%–4.50%. The effective level of the funds rate closed the year at ≈4.3% versus 5.3% when the year began. Unemployment reached 4.2% at the end of November, up approximately 0.50% from the year-earlier level.

Portfolio Performance

During the fourth quarter of 2024, the Harbor Capital Appreciation Fund (Institutional Class, “Fund”) returned 6.22%, underperforming its benchmark, the Russell 1000® Growth Index, which returned 7.07%, and outperforming the S&P 500 Index, which returned 2.41%.

Among the benchmark’s largest sectors, Consumer Discretionary and Communication Services outperformed the broad index. Meanwhile, Information Technology also advanced but underperformed the benchmark. Health Care lost ground during the period.

Stock selection within the Consumer Discretionary, Health Care, and Financials sectors detracted the most from relative performance during the period. Security selection within the Consumer Staples, Information Technology, and Communication Services sectors benefited relative results. Sector allocation effects are residual to our bottom-up process; however, they were a benefit to relative return for the quarter. Our overweights to Consumer Discretionary and Communication Services helped, while our overweight to Health Care detracted.

Contributors & Detractors

Shares in Amazon (AMZN) increased due to optimism about growth in the company’s cloud business and advancements in its artificial intelligence (“AI”)-driven innovations.

Broadcom (AVGO) reported strong revenue and earnings led by AI-networking demand. Shares also benefited from positive expectations of what the company will deliver in the year ahead.

Shares of Eli Lilly (LLY) lost value due to continued challenges in forecasting shorter-term revenue and profits for its weight loss/obesity and diabetes franchises, as well as supply issues.

Shares of Novo Nordisk (NVO) lost value due to disappointing clinical trial results for CagriSema, the company’s latest weight-loss drug.

Buys & Sells

Edwards Lifesciences (EW) is the leader in the global transcatheter valve market. The company offers premium growth in the medical device sector. In the markets where Edwards competes, it has the No. 1 or No. 2 share. The company’s structural heart/valves business continues to grow, and the firm offers innovation within this market. At the same time, non–transcatheter aortic valve replacement technologies are growing fast and becoming a greater contributor to the whole company. Meanwhile, the firm is focusing on expanding its footprint in Asia and Latin America, where cardiovascular diseases are prevalent, potentially capturing new market segments and driving revenue growth.

We eliminated MongoDB (MDB) from the Fund due to the company’s decelerating revenue trajectory and contracting margins.

Overweights & Underweights

Sector weights are driven by our fundamental research-based stock selection. As of December 31, 2024, the Fund’s largest sector weights were Information Technology, Consumer Discretionary, and Communication Services. The largest sector overweights relative to the Russell 1000® Growth Index were in Communication Services and Consumer Discretionary. The biggest underweight relative to the Russell 1000® Growth Index was in Information Technology (due to the very large benchmark weights in Apple, Microsoft, and Nvidia).

Outlook

We exited 2024 on the back of several strong years of share price recovery, particularly for growth stocks, following the sharp declines in late 2021 through 2022. The strength of corporate profits overall has been an important contributor amid sustained growth of the U.S. economy. Employment has remained at healthy levels throughout the period, although with signs of weakening in the past six months. While the rate of inflation has continued to decline, trends have also flattened out in recent months. The path to lower short-term interest rates rests on the dynamics between these factors, and we have less clarity in direction at the end of 2024 than at the beginning of the year.

Against this backdrop, the Fund continues to be anchored on profits growth and longer-term opportunity. All told, we are pleased by the operating results of most of our holdings in 2024. We are also encouraged by the tremendous progress that has been made in the foundational layer of generative AI since the launch of ChatGPT approximately two years ago. Many of the Fund’s best-performing names have benefited from these capital investments, though we are cognizant of the valuation levels reached during the period. Cutbacks of semiconductor holdings over the past year were undertaken with this in mind. However, we remain optimistic about the sector in general and the emerging opportunities to monetize generative AI through software and across industries in the years ahead. As in the previous two years, we continue to see revenue and profits gains for Fund holdings growing at faster rates than the averages against which we compare, while the Fund enjoys a greater degree of diversification and less concentration.

Weighted Average Market Capitalization: The average size of the companies in a portfolio or index as measured by the market value of outstanding shares. Earnings per share is the portion of a company's profit allocated to each outstanding share.

The Est 3-5 Yr EPS Growth (%) is the estimated growth of earnings per share over the next 3-5 years, using pre-calculated mean long-term EPS growth rate estimates, which are calculated using each individual broker’s methodology, from FactSet, First Call, I/B/E/S Consensus, and Reuters. Forward-looking estimates may not come to pass.

% EPS Growth - Past 3 Year: Earnings per share refers to the bottom-line measure of a company’s profitability defined as net income divided by the number of outstanding shares. The Adjusted Trailing P/E (price-to-earnings) Ratio is the closing stock price divided by the sum of the last 12 months actual EPS.

Forecasted P/E Ratio: a measure of the P/E (price-to-earnings) ratio using forecasted earnings for the P/E calculation. Return on Equity (ROE) is a measure of financial performance calculated by dividing net income by shareholders' equity. The Price/Book (price-to-book) Ratio evaluates a firm's market value relative to its book value.

All P/E, ROE and P/B statistics are calculated as weighted medians.

Original Post

data.description:

This video's transcript was generated by a third party. It is not curated or reviewed and is provided for convenience and information purposes only. The accuracy and completeness of the transcript are not guaranteed.

Hey, everyone. I'm Daniel Snyder, and this is ETF Spotlight. Now we are in a complete new market environment for many investors. Now if you're a little bit older, it may not be new to you, but we're talking about the environment where treasuries are actually yielding something again. And if that doesn't get you excited, well, maybe dividends or something else might. But today, we are joined once again by D.J. Tierney from Charles Schwab to talk about the SCHZ fund covering the bond aggregate market. So, obviously, D.J., welcome back to the show. Pleasure to have you.

D.J. Tierney: Thanks, Daniel. Great to see you. Thanks for having me again.

DS: Of course. So we got to dive in. Right? How should investors be looking towards this fund, thinking about how they approach their portfolio, and what would make this maybe that perfect fit for the bond side?

DT: Yeah. So, I mean, the US aggregate bond index is really the highest profile and most tracked taxable index in the bond market. And so SCHZ has got over a decade of experience in delivering those indexed returns. And so what's the role of fixed income in a portfolio? Well, it's to be a ballast and to be an offset versus a lot of the risks in the equity market. So we're big fans of diversified portfolios. And so we think, to your point, now that there's a yield back in the fixed income market, it's a great time to consider, maybe talk to your advisor and figure out where you should be with your fixed income allocation. And for a real basic diversified, taxable fixed income allocation, SCHZ fits the bill. It delivers exposure to treasuries, investment grade corporate bonds, mortgage backed securities, and it does it in a really low cost and tax efficient way.

DS: Yeah. I would hope to talk about that, the metrics, more here in a moment. But people think about aggregate bond, and there are a few of them out there. So what makes Charles Schwab's ETF the go-to destination in your opinion?

DT: Yeah. So we're competitive on cost with an operating expense ratio of three basis points, or 0.03%. I think that's worthy of mention. And then, to your point, there are other aggregate bond ETFs out there. So what I would look to is look to what do you want in a provider? What's their history? How long have they been in the market? Schwab Asset Management, over 15 years. How long has the ETF been in the market? Over a decade. And what is their historical performance in adhering to the index. And if you look at SCHZ on an annual basis, it's tracked that index within 0.1% annualized since inception. So we're -- it's delivering on its mandate, which is to deliver the indexed returns. And then on a tax efficiency basis, we've got a good legacy that's never distributed a capital gain, which is what you would often look for in an ETF. There's never any guarantee of the future, but if it's had that ability to not pay out capital gains, that keeps you in the driver's seat. You'll pay your capital gain on your price appreciation when you sell the ETF, and not from a distribution along the way. So those are some of the things I would point out that make SCHZ worthy of consideration.

DS: Yeah. I'm glad you brought that up. I know we're going to probably show it here on the screen, the graphic, but the tracking errors for this ETF. I mean, A+ grades across the board from the Seeking Alpha Quant system here. Plus, you have over a billion dollars of assets under management in this fund. I mean, that's no small feat, as we're all aware. I think more people are kind of considering though, well, if I go to the bond market and kind of lock this in, I know what the yield is. But if I go to something else like SCHD, as we talked about in the other episode, there is that potential for a little bit of capital appreciation plus yield. So when people are considering that about their portfolio makeup, what is the kind of - is there a specific weighting? Is there an allocation? I mean, I don't know if you can share any of that, but how should investors really be framing the risk-reward scenario here?

DT: Yeah. So it really depends on the investor. What's their demographic? What's their age, time to retirement? Things like that. For SCHZ, this is a bond allocation. And so it's really about providing income and capital preservation. And so if you look at the makeup of this portfolio, over 70% of it is AA rated or higher. So these are on the safer end of the spectrum for taxable fixed income.

And you know your yield, you can calculate your yield on the day you buy it. Of course, your total return over time will depend on the future. Right? If rates go up, that actually could lead to some short-term price depreciation, but then it will actually add your yield over the long term. So, I would say for determining what percentage allocation should you make for fixed income, that's really a great opportunity to talk to an advisor and then consider where you are on your wealth journey.

We have model portfolios where the allocations for something like SCHZ and fixed income will vary a great deal depending on whether you're in your twenties or you're in your sixties. So, but fixed income and SCHZ can really play a key role in terms of being a ballast and offsetting the volatility that's more likely to occur over on the equity side of the ledger.

DS: Yeah. Let's talk metrics real quick. So SCHZ has a dividend yield of 3.95% over the last year and a five-year growth rate of 3.84%. No easy feat. So for the investors that maybe are looking for the highest yield possible, is this for them, or is this more for that person that's just like, I want to sleep well at night. I want to project my future growth and just set it aside.

DT: Yeah. I mean, there's a spectrum of yields in the bond market, and it's all about risk. And if you start with the safest end of the spectrum, that's going to be a low duration treasury, even a treasury bill, right? We're inside of one year where you're going to have minimal interest rate risk because it's short duration, and you're going to have minimal credit risk because you're facing the US government. And then the two metrics that you can really change by moving on the risk spectrum are credit quality. Right? So after treasuries, you might consider mortgage backed securities. You might consider high quality corporate bonds. And then moving along that spectrum, you can all the way - go all the way to high-yield bonds, which have a lower than investment grade rating. So you can increase your yield by increasing your risk on that spectrum. And then the same thing in terms of maturity in a normally shaped curve, which we, you know, we're getting back to again where yields go up over time. The 30-year treasury yields more than the 10-year treasury, which yields more than the 5-year treasury. You can increase your yield by moving out, but that's not without risk. You get interest rate risk when you move out.

So SCHZ is kind of a nice right down the middle where you've got a combination of treasuries, mortgage backed securities, and corporate bonds all mixed together. And then in terms of maturity, it's kind of in that intermediate part of the maturity curve where it's not out 10 years, beyond 10 years. It rests in that intermediate part of the curve, closer to five years in terms of duration. So we think it's a nice proxy, to give you a glimpse of the overall taxable bond market and just a really convenient way. Can you imagine, you're getting exposure to thousands of bonds just by buying one equity ticker? And it's just it really it's been a great industry development to give this kind of low-cost diversification and easy access to bonds. And that's really the big win with bond ETFs like SCHZ.

DS: I will say my personal opinion. It is a lot easier. I don't know if anybody watching has used the US TreasuryDirect website, but, man, it needs a little updating. So I want to talk obviously about the turnover asset ratio. 63%. Some investors might look at that and go, well, if they're buying these and holding them for so long, why is there so much turnover? What can you possibly shed light on that?

DT: Yeah. I mean, it's one thing to keep in mind is that, the bond market, you every day you go on, you approach maturity, right? So a part of the process here is bonds walk down that maturity, and they fall less than one year, they'll drop out of the aggregate bond index. And so if they're not in the index, we'll look to either sell them or redeem them out of the fund in a tax efficient way, and then we'll go buy more bonds. The corporate bond market issues a trillion dollars in new corporate bonds every year. And as they come into the market, come into the index, we'll want that exposure. You know, same thing, mortgage backed bonds, you might have prepayments. And so, really, it's just representing what the bond market's doing. And again, why might you be worried about turnover? Well, you might be worried about generating capital gains. Again, SCHZ, since inception, has never delivered a capital gain. So your money stays in the market, receiving that income and then potentially getting some capital appreciation as well. So a tax efficient way to do it, and the turnover again is just representative of what the bond market does every year.

DS: I would like to take a second to try to dive in with you if you don't mind. You're saying mortgage backed securities. You're saying corporate bonds. People like to think about risk, obviously, as we've been talking about, and treasuries are considered that risk-free rate. But then you start to look at the ratings of the bonds and the mortgage backed securities. But on your side, kind of painting this picture that this fund is walking the middle of the line. Now does that mean that you're only focused on more like AA, AAA bonds and things like that, or is it kind of diverse approach?

DT: It's a diverse approach. So it's going to represent what's in the investment grade market. So, investment grade can go all the way down to BBBs. But what you'll see with this is that, and I think I gave a stat, over 70% of the portfolio is AA or higher. Now, that's partially represented by about half the fund is U.S. Treasury or government agency securities, which are at the AAA level. But in the investment grade space, it can go all the way down to BBB, but you'll see BBBs make up right around 12% of the fund. So again, a vast majority is going to be A or better. So again, it's going to represent the -- we're not making a call here. We are just representing the market as issuance wide levels at the index level.

DS: Yeah. Thank you so much. Now, as you mentioned already, three basis points of expense ratio. I mean, I want to say for all investors, thank you to Charles Schwab for keeping expenses so low because I know we all focus on that. But as this market goes forward, let's paint one last picture here. As this market goes forward, there have been whispers of pushing rate cuts out, right, or potentially even a rate hike depending on what happens. We're watching tariff wars. We're seeing all this stuff happening with the administration. What are the thoughts over there at Schwab and the team about how this ETF might be affected by what's to come?

DT: Yeah. So we have a Schwab Center for Financial Research, which consists of people that just do what you said, look at the market and really try to make calls. And so Kathy Jones is our fixed income strategist for Schwab Center for Financial Research, SCFR, we like to call it. And she's constructive on investment grade, and she's even constructive on the absolute level of yields right now. And again, we don't have a crystal ball. So we don't know where the 10-year treasury or corporate bonds will be yielding in a year from now. But based on the quality of balance sheets for aggregate corporate bond issuers, I think she feels like they're in a pretty good place. And then just on in terms of a historical yield play spectrum, your entry point right now with the 10-year treasury north, if you get into the market when treasuries are between 4.5%, high 4%, that generally has served you pretty well. And again, with corporate bonds adding incremental yield to that, we think if rates stay in this spectrum of kind of 4%, 4.5%, 4.75% it's a reasonable time to get into the market where you've got some cushion against volatility with rates moving forward.

DS: DJ, let's go ahead and leave it there. Thank you so much for jumping on the program. Again, everyone, the Schwab US Aggregate Bond ETF, SCHZ. Go ahead and click the follow on the symbol page here on Seeking Alpha. DJ, we look forward to the next episode. We hope you take care, and we'll see you here soon. Alright?

DT: Thanks, Daniel. Bye bye.

data.title: goeasy: High ROEs And Loan Growth Can Continue (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Please note all $ figures are in $CAD, not $USD, unless otherwise stated.

Introduction

goeasy (TSX:GSY:CA) just reported their latest Q4’24 results and performance came in better than expected. As a leader in alternative lending in Canada, the company is eying continued increases in its loan portfolio with attractive ROEs and operating margins as the company lends well in excess of their cost of capital. This piece will be a follow-up to my previous coverage on goeasy where I’ll dive deeper into the company’s latest quarterly results and assess the outlook going forward. I’ll also provide my thoughts on the valuation and explain why I think now is the time to be a buyer of the stock.

goeasy is executing on top-line growth

goeasy concluded 2024 with a strong fourth quarter, achieving record financial results on both the top and bottom line. On revenues, the company had a record $405.2 million in revenue, which surpassed last year’s quarter by 19.8%. While bad debts rose by 40.9% year over year, the company’s surge in credit applications and minimal operating expense increase were enough to drive EBITDA margin expansion as EBITDA grew 21.3% year over year to $184.3 million. On net income, the company recorded a 12.2% increase which represented an 11.0% increase compared to last year on EPS, with EPS of $4.45 beating sellside estimates by 8 cents.

When we look at what drove goeasy’s performance this quarter, much of the trends we saw play out in the full year carried over. During FY’24, the company issued over 315k loans to 1.5 million customers. For Q4’24 specifically, loan originations climbed 15% year over year to clock in at $813.7 million, mostly driven by unsecured lending growth. The biggest contributors to this were car financing and home equity lending, which increased 30%+

The last time I reviewed goeasy, the company had been benefitting from the rise of alternative lending as consumers perceived their financial situations as having been worse compared to Q2’23. In the latest ‘Canadian Survey of Consumer Expectations’ for Q4’24, one of the key takeaways was that consumers were feeling better about their overall financial health, especially with rate cuts ahead. In 2024, Canadian consumers benefitted from five rate cuts that brought down the overnight rate from 5.00% to 3.25% by the end of 2024. This was in response to softening economic activities, easing inflation, and to support economic growth.

In terms of what we’re seeing now, I think it’s fair to say that improved consumer sentiment is due to lower financial stress, as most consumers benefitted from a declining interest rate environment. At the same time, however, the CSCE index of financial health (shown below) illustrates that while we’ve seen an improvement in sentiment, this is still below pre-pandemic levels.

This has both positive and negative consequences for goeasy. On one hand, the company is set to benefit from rising spending as consumers start spending more. As illustrated below, most consumers expect that their expenses on things like durables, vacations, restaurants, essential products, and housing costs are set to rise. Rising prices means more dollars spent which in theory means more loan origination organically. On the other hand, it could signal that as credit becomes easier to access than before, there is going to be less of a need for alternative lending.

From a profitability perspective, I think goeasy continues to underwrite profitability, maintaining good operating margins and ROEs. In the latest quarter, the company’s efficiency ratio decreased by 410bps to 24.2% which helped to operate income climb 20% year over year to $168 million.

In recent quarters, there has been discussion around the maximum interest rate companies like goeasy can charge to consumers. This rate is set by the federal government and, at present, that maximum sits at 35%. This is still quite high in absolute terms, and that’s allowed goeasy to maintain 25.9% ROE in the latest quarter; however, this is down about 80bps compared to last year’s Q4.

As for the balance sheet, goeasy currently has funding capacity of $1.9 billion, with a weighted average cost of borrowing of 6.8%. With the Canadian 5-year government yield at 2.75%, this is a pretty large spread, however, given that goeasy charges 20%+ on their loans, they earn a significant spread that compensates them for the higher credit risk consumers they lend to.

Flipping to bullish considering the outlook

My last two articles on goeasy have been ‘hold’ recommendations, which have probably been the right call as the stock has been rangebound over the last year or so. However, I think now is the time to get bullish on the stock given the improving fundamentals around the business.

Given that the company just wrapped up its year-end, management provided a fair bit of guidance for the medium term. In its new three-year forecast, the company is now guiding for organic growth of the loan portfolio to reach $7.35 billion to $7.75 billion by 2027, from the current $4.60 billion today.

I think there are two takeaways from this guidance. Firstly, management is guiding for continued growth. At the midpoint of their 2027 targeted range, the loan portfolio should still grow about 18% annually on average, indicating that current capitalization, leverage and positioning should allow them to achieve this organically. Secondly, ROEs are expected to contract going forward. From the current 25.9% this quarter, management sees ROEs coming down to no less than 23% over the next three years.

In my view, I think there’s reason to have confidence in these targets. For one thing, 2024 represents the 15th year of consistent same store revenue growth, highlighting that goeasy has been able to grow in both good markets and bad ones too. In addition, the company’s 2024 targets were achieved with relative ease. Across all guidance metrics, goeasy either achieved or surpassed their guidance, which, I think, should lend confidence to their ability to forecast business results going forward. Management also noted on their earnings call that their guidance does reflect accounts for potential headwinds, as well as the implementation of the 35% maximum allowable interest rate that they can charge to borrowers.

Regulatory and macro risks should be watched

One of the key risks to goeasy’s business model lies in the regulatory landscape. The implementation of a 35% maximum allowable interest rate as discussed on the call could significantly impact goeasy's profitability. I have no idea what the right interest rate should be, but it should be noted that the 35% level is still higher than what’s typically allowed in the United States.

It can vary state by state and in Florida, for example, the maximum rate is 25%. Some states defer this to contract law instead of usury law, which makes it more complicated, and interest rates charged to consumers can also be governed by what type of collateral is pledged (if any), but rates are typically higher in Canada than in most parts of the U.S. If the federal government deemed the current 35% maximum to be predatory or usurious, this could be a major headwind for goeasy’s business model. While management plans to mitigate this by reducing borrowing costs and passing along rate reductions, the extent of this impact and the company's ability to fully offset it remain uncertain.

Outside of this, the macro environment should be a top consideration. As noted on the conference call by management, a weakening economy could lead to increased delinquency and credit losses despite the company's proactive credit tightening measures. While goeasy has a good track record of managing credit risk during prolonged economic downturns and a weaker economic environment generally lends itself towards more alternative lending, this is still a risk to watch as it relates directly to net charge offs which ultimately drives profitability of loans.

goeasy is trading at a reasonable price

Analysts are bullish on the near-term outlook for goeasy’s stock. As shown below, there are currently 7 ‘buy’ ratings with 2 ‘hold’ ratings. With an average price target of $239.89, analysts see about 37.6% upside from the current price (source: Bloomberg).

On valuation, goeasy trades at an inexpensive multiple relative to peers. Compared to other companies in the alternative lending space, goeasy trades at a discount at 8.5x P/E compared to peer group that trades at 9.9x (source: Bloomberg). Given the higher ROEs, faster growth rates, and the fact that Canada is a better operating environment because of the higher rates one can charge, I think goeasy is one of the better names in the space. At a 14% discount to the company’s five-year average multiple, I’m seeing more compelling value in the stock today than I was a year ago.

Takeaway

In conclusion, goeasy's Q4’24 results highlighted its continue growth and profitability even in a slightly more challenged operating environment. Despite potential risks from regulatory changes and a slowing economy, I believe goeasy is well-positioned to navigate these challenges given its strong track record and expanding loan portfolio. The stock is currently trading at an attractive valuation, with a P/E multiple of 8.5x, well below the industry average of 9.9x and with its high ROEs, continued loan growth, and a favorable operating environment in Canada, I think the stock is a compelling buy at this valuation.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: JGRO: Not As Actively Managed As It May Seem, Yet Upside Remains Possible
data.description:
This article emphasises a risk-return attribution analysis of the JPMorgan Active Growth ETF (NYSEARCA:JGRO), an active fund that follows a bottom-up fundamental approach to provide its investors with an excess return mandate.

The JPMorgan Active Growth ETF, otherwise known as JGRO, has delivered comprehensive returns since its inception. However, as with most active ETFs, continuous risk-return attribution is required to determine whether the vehicle would contribute to an investor's portfolio optimisation journey.

Without further ado, here are our latest findings on JGRO.

What Is JGRO?

JGRO is an actively-managed exchange-traded fund that emphasises stock selection more than index tracking. Moreover, the vehicle is pro-growth stock, meaning it has a natural tilt towards growth as a systematic factor.

Although it primarily invests in large-to-mega cap stocks, JGRO is unrestricted, providing it with the latitude to invest in smaller capitalisation stocks if needed. In addition to its broad-based style and security exposure, JGRO occasionally uses derivatives to achieve its desired equity exposure, which is clever as derivatives provide a useful tool for rebalancing without liquidating underlying holdings at unfavourable prices or during illiquid market environments.

Market Commentary

Here's an overview of the fund's exposure, its outlook, and what we think about it all, looking ahead.

Current Exposure

Although not a tracker fund, JGRO uses the Russell 1000 Growth Index as a performance benchmark, and therefore it shares similar exposure. JGRO has around 111 constituents, meaning it will naturally have active return exposure versus the index; however, as shown later in the article, JGRO and the Russell 1000 Growth Index have common risk-return features.

Illustrated below is a chart of the fund's current exposure, showing commitment to information technology, communication services, and consumer discretionary goods. On the other end of the spectrum, JGRO has negligible exposure to energy, consumer staples, and materials.

The ETF is security diversified with none of its top holdings exceeding 8% of the total portfolio market value. Nevertheless, evidence shows commitment to stocks such as Amazon (AMZN), NVIDIA (NVDA), Apple (AAPL), Meta (META), Alphabet (GOOGL) (GOOG), Microsoft (MSFT), Tesla (TSLA), Mastercard (MA), Broadcom (AVGO), and Netflix (NFLX). Similar to its sector exposure, JGRO's top ten security-specific exposures is quite tech orientated.

Outlook

A glance at the fund's recent market commentary illustrates a somewhat contrarian narrative. JGRO's management highlighted a recent reduction of information technology and AI bets in its portfolio to reduce concentration risk and to capitalise on a decoupling between fundamental variables and market sentiment. Moreover, the fund highlighted that idiosyncratic opportunities have emerged in the financial and industrial sectors.

What do we make of all this?

Here's a breakdown of our stance.

  1. We like the idea of a reduction in IT exposure. While growth seems robust, the IT sector possesses a Shiller P/E Ratio of around 59.4x versus the S&P 500's average of 38.5x. Aside from relative valuation concerns, the AI boom seems hyped up and likely vulnerable to overzealous investors. However, as a contra argument, nearly 35% of JGRO's portfolio remains exposed to IT bets, suggesting portfolio concentration remains a noteworthy risk.
  2. We like the idea of increasing exposure to the financial sector. As discussed in a previous article, we are bullish on U.S. banking (link here) and we see value in insurance companies such as UnitedHealth (UNH) (link here).
  3. Low exposure to energy and real estate is understandable given that they're lower-growth sectors, which depart from the ETF's growth theme.

Lastly, I'd like to outline a style-based argument.

A factor-based analysis shows that a reduction in the U.S. 10-year yield leads to higher returns for growth stocks. Moreover, it's proven that larger cap stocks usually outperform small-cap when the 10-Y decreases. Given the current flattening and downlevelling of the 10-Y curve, this theory aligns JGRO with interim upside. However, caution must be attributed to the fact that yield curves shift for various reasons, and that an inversion of the curve might act contra to the aforementioned theory by denting growth stocks.

Risk-Return Attribution

I gathered a few metrics from a risk-return attribution analysis. The results were benchmarked to the Russell 1000 Growth Index, and to add texture, I used the same benchmark and compared the iShares Russell 1000 ETF's (IWB) results.

A discussion of the metrics follow the next diagram.

Metric JGRO IWB IWF
Active Return -0.41% -6.30% N/A
Active Risk 3.10% 6.13% N/A
Information Ratio -0.13 -1.03 N/A
Sharpe Ratio 1.21 1 1.24
Sortino Ratio 2.14 1.66 2.11
Skewness -0.45 -0.53 -0.65%
Conditional VaR -7.75% 7.46% 8.70%

Source: Portfolio Visualiser

JGRO has a negative active return of -0.41%, suggesting it underperforms its benchmark by a slight amount. Moreover, it has an active risk (or tracking error) of 3.10%, suggesting it has higher risk than its benchmark. Collectively, these metrics translate into an Information Ratio (active return/active risk) of -0.14, which we don't deem ideal as it shows inferior active risk-return to its benchmark.

Furthermore, JGRO has a Sharpe Ratio of 1.21, suggesting it provides positive risk-adjusted excess returns. Unlike active returns, excess returns are measured relative to a risk free rate, which is a key consideration in this instance. In our view, despite ranking lower than IWF's Sharpe Ratio, JGRO has a comprehensive Sharpe Ratio, which conveys favourable volatility-adjusted returns, especially compared to IWB.

Similar to its Sharpe Ratio, JGRO has a positive Sortino Ratio. The Sortino Ratio measures excess returns (actual return - an investor determined minimum return) relative to downside deviation, painting a picture of downside volatility risk. Despite sitting three points below IWF, JGRO comfortably surpasses IWB's Sortino Ratio.

Lastly, JGRO has better Sortino, Skewness and Conditional Value-at-Risk figures than IWB and IWF, suggesting it possesses less tail risk. For those unaware, here are the explanations of Skewness and CVaR.

So, Are JGRO's Risk-Return Attributes Favourable?

If we isolate performance against JGRO's benchmark (the IWF) and ignore its performance relative to IWB, metrics show that a juxtaposition exists. For example, JGRO provides negative Information and Sharpe Ratios suggesting its security selection might be inferior from an excess return standpoint. However, JGRO's Skewness and Conditional VaR are both better than IWF's, implying superior tail risk mitigation – Keep in mind: Minimising drawdowns contribute to total returns, it's not just about Seeking Alpha in bull markets.

To end this section, time series data shows that IWF has delivered minor outperformance since JGRO's inception, aligning with our above-mentioned findings. The minor underperformance doesn't mean JGRO is a worse vehicle. As illustrated earlier, its downside risk metrics are superior, meaning a selection between JGRO and its underlying index is a matter of preference for investors.

Aside: Note that IWF is a vehicle managed by iShares that tracks the Russell 1000 Growth Index. As such, a proxy was used for JGRO's benchmark as opposed to a primary reference. Here's a link to true index Vs. vehicle performance.

Other Metrics

JGRO has an expense ratio of 0.44%, which is 25 basis points higher than IWF's expense Ratio. This is likely due to higher active management, nonetheless, it is a consideration worth noting. Although it possesses a higher expense ratio, JGRO has a much smaller AUM base, allowing it to be nimble, especially during periods where portfolio rebalancing and/or reconstitution is required.

Neither vehicle's dividend yields are particularly compelling, which, in fairness, won't be the case for growth vehicles because favourable dividend attributes are usually found in mature stocks instead of growth stocks. Whether JGRO's relative yield disadvantage of 25 basis points is important depends on investor-to-investor, especially as both vehicles aren't geared toward yield-seeking investors.

Final Word

The JPMorgan Active Growth ETF's active return strategy probably overstates the amount of active risk it assumes. Moreover, the vehicle's risk-return attribution shows inferior upside performance relative to its benchmark, in turn, raising questions about the fund's security selection process.

On a positive note, the JPMorgan Active Growth ETF shows signs of better downside protection than its benchmark. Moreover, we concur with JPMorgan Active Growth ETF's market outlook as we foresee a divergence between market sentiment and fundamental worth, especially given the possible overcommitment to artificial intelligence investments.

In essence, we deem this vehicle's prospects borderline. While it presents docile active activity, index underperformance, and an elevated expense ratio, we think JGRO deserves a soft 'Buy' rating, in light of it being suitable to some investors and given the likely pro-large cap growth systematic environment.

data.title: Advanced Energy Industries: Rating Upgrade Warranted After A Decent Recovery
data.description:
The Thesis

As Advanced Energy Industries (NASDAQ:AEIS) exited 2024, it finally saw growth across its consolidated top line after more than a year of experiencing decline, thanks to robust double-digit growth in the data center and semiconductor end market. While the growth across the industrial and telecom segments is expected to remain subdued in the short term due to volume pressure from elevated inventory levels at distributors and customers, I expect a healthy demand environment in data center and semiconductor businesses, and contributions from recent acquisitions should continue to support top-line growth in the quarters ahead. For margin, I believe, volume leverage and focus on improving manufacturing cost should be key drivers for growth in quarters ahead, which along with an improving mix should result in margin expansion in 2025 and beyond.

Since my neutral stance in October last year, the stock price has been up in the low teens. However, since then, the stock's valuation has also seen a notable improvement, supported by strong bottom-line performance in the past quarter, as the company experienced significant volume growth across most of its business, outperforming expectations leading to top-line and margin growth. Considering these factors and the company’s promising long-term prospects, I see it as a solid investment opportunity after a prolonged period of underperformance. Therefore, I am upgrading the stock to a buy at current levels for long-term investors.

Last Quarter Highlights and Outlook

After experiencing contraction for six consecutive quarters, the company’s consolidated top line finally grew as it exited 2024. The company’s top line was up approximately 2.5% year on year to $415 million in the fourth quarter of the year, primarily due to robust double-digit growth in its data center and computing, and Semiconductor Equipment segment. While the data center segment expanded nearly 41% versus the prior year to record $89 million, AEIS’s core semiconductor business grew 18.5% year on year, thanks to strong demand and shipment levels during the quarter.

However, the Industrial and Telcom segment remains a headwind for the company, which, yet again, reported a double-digit year-on-year decline in its revenue during the quarter. For the full year, the consolidated top line was down 10.5% primarily due to weakness across most parts of the business in the initial quarters of 2024.

Although the top line was up in the low single digits, the company’s margins were strong as its adjusted operating margin expanded 120 bps year on year to 13.5% during the last quarter. This growth was primarily a result of strong volumes in certain parts of the business, improved factory efficiency, and a better mix. Operating profit growth also benefited the company's bottom-line performance as its adjusted EPS increased to $1.30 from $1.24 a year ago, beating the consensus estimates by a notable $0.20 in the fourth quarter of 2024.

The company has been experiencing weakness across all its segments since the last couple of quarters, leading to a decline in its top line. However, exiting the year 2024, the company's top-line growth turned positive due to robust growth in its data center and semiconductor business outperforming expectations, thanks to strong demand across customers. I expect this growth to continue further into FY25 as the demand environment remains robust, particularly in the company’s data center and semiconductor segments, due to continued investments in AI-driven infrastructure.

While the semiconductor business is experiencing strong customer pull due to its best-in-class products like eVoS, eVerest, and NavX, the increasing demand for AI computing and strength in hyperscale should continue to drive demand for the company’s solution in its data center business. In my view, continued strength in these parts of the business, and contribution from the recent acquisition of Airity, a power technology company, should support top-line growth in the quarters ahead.

The company’s industrial and medical segment, which accounts for nearly 21% of the total revenue, remains under pressure as its customers and distributors continue to work down excess inventories. However, the design win activity was robust in this segment, as it won new designs in test and measurement due to a healthy stream of new products and an improved go-to-market strategy, which, in my view, should support the segment's revenue growth in 2025.

In addition, the company continues to invest in its R&D to develop and launch new next-generation products for evolving customer needs. Further, the recent acquisitions have significantly expanded the company's offerings, benefitting the company's market presence. Going ahead, the company continues to actively pursue strategic acquisitions to strengthen its market position through enhanced capabilities in semiconductor power solutions, data center infrastructure, and industrial applications and remains focused on targeting acquisitions that enhance its market position and drive share gains across geographies and end markets.

Additionally, the company continues to invest in R&D to develop and launch next-generation products that cater to evolving customer needs. Recent acquisitions have also significantly expanded its product offerings, strengthening its market presence. Going ahead, AEIS remains committed to pursuing strategic acquisitions that enhance its capabilities in semiconductor power solutions, data center infrastructure, and industrial applications, which, I believe, should bolster its market position and drive share gains across key geographies and end markets.

The company’s financials are also sound with nearly a $350 million reduction in net debt versus the prior year levels, which has significantly improved its leverage ratio. In my view, this improved leverage along with plenty of liquidity with about $722.1 million in cash should support the company’s strategic acquisition in the long term, fueling the company’s top-line growth in the coming years.

Overall, while the strong demand environment in the data center and semiconductor business is expected to continue into 2025, the industrial and medical business also appears to be bottoming out, as evidenced by robust design activity that the company experienced in the last quarter, which should further support consolidated top-line growth in the quarters ahead. On the other hand, long-term tailwinds primarily from rising investments in AI-driven infrastructure and the company's focus on expanding its market reach and strengthening its position through strategic M&A should drive top-line growth in the coming years.

Valuation

I last covered AEIS in October 2024 with a neutral stance due to an unfavorable short-term growth outlook and an expensive valuation. Since, then, the stock has been up about 14% after the recent surge in the stock post Q4'24 earnings report, outperforming the market estimates. Currently, the company's stock is trading at a forward non-GAAP P/E ratio of 25.77, based on FY25 EPS estimates of $4.82, representing a year-on-year increase of 29.99%. While this stock appears to be valued above its historical average of 21.32x, it has improved significantly since my last article due to strong bottom-line growth and is expected to continue further into 2025.

Going ahead, I expect robust demand, particularly in the data center and computing and semiconductor end markets, to continue to support the company's revenue growth in the quarters ahead. This, along with healthy shipment levels in the semiconductor business and robust design wins in the industrial and medical segment, should drive overall top-line growth in FY25. The company's margins have been under pressure for the last few quarters, primarily due to weak volumes across the business. However, as volumes have started to recover, I believe the company's focus on manufacturing cost improvements and an improved mix should support margin growth in FY25 and beyond. The company has also reduced its net debt significantly by about $350 million, decreasing the interest burden notably. In my view, with an expected growth in profitability, the benefit from reduced interest expenses should support the bottom-line growth in the quarters ahead, further enhancing the stock's valuation.

Risk

Since my first coverage of AEIS, the company has seen a significant improvement across both top and bottom lines. However, this improvement was majorly driven by the data center and semiconductor businesses of the company. Whereas, other parts of the business, including industrial and telecom, which collectively account for nearly 28% share in total revenue, remain under pressure due to normalization after robust growth in 2023 and elevated inventories at customers and distributors. While I expect the company's top line to see decent improvement from here on after multiple quarters of consecutive declines, the pressure across some parts of the business remains a potential risk that could impact the overall performance in the quarters ahead, potentially causing further underperformance of the stock in the near term.

Conclusion

As discussed above, the company's stock is trading slightly above its historical average. The company's revenue growth is expected to be driven by continued healthy demand for the company's products and solutions, particularly in the data center and semiconductor end market, in the quarters ahead. With anticipated volume growth and benefits from the company's focus on improving efficiency and better mix, it should support margin expansion in 2025. Considering these factors and a promising long-term outlook driven by the increasing market share and focus on expanding offerings according to evolving end customer needs, I am upgrading AEIS stock to a "buy" rating.

data.title: 80% Of Americans Are On The Verge Of Retirement Failure
data.description:
Co-authored by Treading Softly.

"Only a crisis - actual or perceived - produces real change. When that crisis occurs, the actions that are taken depend on the ideas that are lying around. That, I believe, is our basic function: to develop alternatives to existing policies, to keep them alive and available until the politically impossible becomes the politically inevitable." – Milton Friedman.

How much do you love change?

If you're honest, most of us despise change. We want things to stay as similar as possible, simply because we would rather not deal with a new reality or paradigm. It's been famously said that the more things change, the more they stay the same. For some people, that's true. They adapt small parts of their life to change, but otherwise try to keep everything level and even-keeled.

The mid-90s brought many changes to the idea of planning for retirement. One of the most significant changes that arrived was when Bill Bengen crafted his rule of a 4% withdrawal rate. Even though it was designed to provide a steady flow of income over a 30-year retirement, it still had a 5% failure rate. This means that 5% of all retirees would run out of money before they ran out of breath.

1993 was when the first low-cost index funds started to become available. Their popularity rapidly expanded in the early 2000s, becoming a primary means of retirement savings for many. This first fund was the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY), which actively tracked the S&P 500 (SP500).

Before this, many mutual funds were used; even earlier, retirees or investors who were saving for retirement primarily bought into blue-chip companies, which would overwhelmingly pay dividends. The popularity of dividends rapidly declined as the means of investing in the market shifted over to an ETF-first focus.

This meant that your investments in the market were less likely to pay you money, forcing you to sell to unlock your capital.

Let's see what this new focus has led to.

The 80% At Risk

According to the National Council on Aging, 80% of older households are financially struggling or at risk of financial crisis. This is a massive number because previously it recognized that 50% of all Americans were failing to save for retirement properly.

What we're seeing is the result of the second statistic pouring into the first. If you fail to save for retirement properly, you will be at a greater risk of financial struggle or on the edge of financial calamity in retirement.

Previously, retirement thought was built around a three-pronged approach: a government-supplied pension via Social Security, a privately provided pension via your workplace, and then your retirement savings and investments as it's meant to be a fallback should the other two not be enough. In recent history, we have seen that the second leg has mostly disappeared; instead of a pension plan from your employer, you have a 401k, which is just an enlargement of the third leg. It would be unfair to say that Social Security has diminished in importance; a vast majority of retirees are over-reliant on that first leg. Social Security payouts, when compared to the 1950s, are significantly higher than they used to be, on average, even when adjusted for inflation.

It shouldn't be missed that the greatest degree of retirement savings decline coincides with a shift in the method by which we plan for retirement.

So, what is causing this growing calamity?

The 90% Seeing Lost Wealth

The biggest issue with pegging your retirement success upon the success of your savings, especially your investments, is that any market downturn can cause massive issues.

Between 2014 and 2016, 90% of retirees saw a falling net worth and lower income, even though the market performed very strongly:

401ks became exceptionally popular in the 1980s. This was largely spawned by the 1978 Revenue Act, which allowed pre-tax money to be used and led to the downfall of private-sector pensions. The argument was that if they could incentivize individuals to save more through 401k, then they could take care of themselves in retirement, instead of having to become a financial burden on companies. Companies like General Motors (GM) and Ford (F) have struggled under the weight of their pension commitments, and other companies completely shirk their pensions through bankruptcy. The weight of the commitment to their prior employees overwhelmed their ability to perform profitably. This fear that this would occur led to the promotion of 401k plans.

Unfortunately, the 401k plan also led to a steady decline in effective retirement savings. Many employees did not opt into those plans, and many employees who opted into them didn't fully understand them.

What formed was a perfect storm that only was compounded in 1993.

In 1993, we had the formation of the first passive market-wide ETF, SPY, followed by the 4% withdrawal rule, which made an easy rule of thumb in the financial retirement planning world to help retirees last at least 30 years in retirement. It stunted retirement planning and growth because so many advisers simply blindly follow this now over thirty-year-old methodology. Although attempts have been made to adjust or adapt it, such as recommending the withdrawal rate not be based on original portfolio value but current value, these attempts have failed to shift retirement thinking.

The issue, though, was that most retirees were not saving enough to see their retirement funds grow to sufficient size for the 4% material rule to provide sufficient income and retirement. Either they had to withdraw more in risk of a greater chance of economic calamity down the road, especially in their most sensitive years as an elder, or they had to drastically change their lifestyle due to their plan failure.

The change of focus away from receiving a steady income from the market to selling shares to unlock cash was also quietly promoted by corporate management teams. It was in the '90s, the same time frame that we saw the market-wide ETFs arrive, that companies started to go away from paying large dividends. I think this quote sums it up well:

"During the 1990s, dividends were de-emphasized. At the time, companies thought they were better able to deploy their capital by reinvesting it in their businesses rather than returning it to shareholders. Significant capital appreciation year in and year out caused investors to shift their attention away from dividends."

This created a perfect storm of misunderstanding caused by requiring a massive paradigm shift for retirees. Instead of receiving biweekly or monthly paychecks and being told to live within their means, retirees were forced to sell their savings and to survive off those. Because pension plans had fallen by the wayside, they were no longer receiving their monthly pension checks. Social Security checks were not enough to sustain them. Their savings had been enlarged into the 401k plans, meaning more and more retirees were forced into the delicate balance of selling off their savings to survive, hoping they didn't run out before they passed away.

Because retirees and working individuals didn't understand the new methodology, being promoted from the 1990s forward led to a greater and greater decline in retirement savings. We are now at a pinnacle point in which retirees have the least amount saved ever, and those who are still working are continuing to make this trend worse.

The 100% Seeing Income Growth

The solution that I see for this problem is simple.

It's the reason that I spent decades crafting and refining my unique Income Method that we use within High Dividend Opportunities. Before I was born, retirees were using a methodology very similar to what I promote and very similar to what many people now scorn because they've never been taught about it.

Investing for dividends to receive a recurring income from the market was exceptionally popular in the 1940s, 1950s, and 1970s. During those years, the market performed ho-hum, and the overall return for retirees was primarily in the form of dividends.

Yet, being paid large dividends from the market shouldn't be considered foreign or unusual. The first dividend recorded on a modern stock exchange was back in 1602. The Dutch East India Company, trading on the Amsterdam Stock Exchange, paid an annual dividend equivalent to 18% of its total market value and continued to pay similarly sized annual dividends for nearly 200 years.

The U.S. government has created and regulated specific types of companies that allow you to receive a large sum of income from them so they can avoid paying federal tax. RICs, or registered investment companies, span a wide range of investments from REITs (Real Estate Investment Trusts) to BDCs (Business Development Companies) to CEFs (Closed-End Funds) and ETFs (Exchange-Traded Funds). The market is awash with options and opportunities to invest in it and receive a recurring livable income. In essence, you can use the market to create your own private pension. Then, along with Social Security, you can live within your means, enabling you to have a retirement that isn't dependent upon the ebbs and flows of the market. Perhaps it's time for you to take away the 30-year-old methodology that is failing millions of retirees and pick up a methodology that has existed for over 400 years. If dividend investing sounds brand new to you, it's because you've only looked at the last 30 years of investors' thought processes and stock market performance; we need to look beyond it to the years of retirement thinking from earlier on instead. We need to take the idea that what is more moderate and new is better and recognize that it is failing. It might be new and flashy, but like many things we see today, the new ways we'd build things result in poor products with poor quality without the longevity of former methods.

If you're tired of cheap products on the shelves that break within a few months, then you should be tired of lazy retirement planning that causes so many to fail. Build a portfolio of quality dividend-paying companies that pay you livable yields.

That's the beauty of my Income Method. That's the beauty of income investing.
